elements.sent-2411-001: Fine-tuning multimodal large language models (MLLMs) presents significant challenges, including a reliance on high-level visual features that limits fine-grained detail comprehension, and data conflicts that arise from task complexity.
elements.sent-2411-002: To address these issues, we propose an efficient fine-tuning framework with two novel approaches: Vision Cue Enhancement (VCE) and Dual Low-Rank Adaptation (Dual-LoRA).
elements.sent-2411-003: VCE enhances the vision projector by integrating multi-level visual cues, improving the model’s ability to capture fine-grained visual features.
elements.sent-2411-004: Dual-LoRA introduces a dual low-rank structure for instruction tuning, decoupling learning into skill and task spaces to enable precise control and efficient adaptation across diverse tasks.
elements.sent-2411-005: Our method simplifies implementation, enhances visual comprehension, and improves adaptability.
elements.sent-2411-006: Experiments on both downstream tasks and general benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed approach.
elements.sent-2411-007: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have demonstrated impressive capabilities across a wide range of tasks, showcasing versatility and effectiveness in diverse applications.
elements.sent-2411-008: Existing MLLMs~\cite{liu2023llava,bai2023qwen_vl,liu2024llava1.5 typically integrate pretrained Large Language Models (LLMs) \cite{touvron2023llama,chiang2023vicuna with pretrained vision encoders \cite{radford2021clip,liu2023dino , using a vision projector to connect visual features to the LLMs.}}}
elements.sent-2411-009: The standard training process for MLLMs generally involves two stages: (1) a vision projector pretraining stage, where only the vision projector is trained to align visual features for the LLMs, and (2) an instruction tuning stage, where both the vision projector and the LLM are trained to perform visual instruction tasks.
elements.sent-2411-010: To reduce the extensive number of parameters in LLM tuning, low-rank adaptation (LoRA) \cite{hu2021lora was proposed by injecting lightweight adapters into pretrained LLMs for efficient fine-tuning.}
elements.sent-2411-011: The vision projector pretraining aligns visual and language feature spaces, while instruction tuning equips MLLMs to generate responses aligned with human instructions.
elements.sent-2411-012: The goal of these two stages is to equip MLLMs with sufficient visual cues and to ensure accurate instruction-based responses.
elements.sent-2411-013: Despite the great success, existing MLLMs have two main limitations.
elements.sent-2411-014: On the one hand, in the vision projector pretraining stage, current methods primarily rely on high-level visual features, often overlooking low-level and fine-grained details, which limits visual comprehension.
elements.sent-2411-015: For example, most works\cite{liu2024llava1.5,cha2024honeybee use only the high-level semantic feature map for vision token projection, while some \cite{tong2024cambrian1,yao2024dense leverage multi-layer visual features yet still miss finer details.}}
elements.sent-2411-016: On the other hand, in the instruction-tuning stage, as downstream tasks grow in diversity and complexity, data conflicts in LoRA-based instruction tuning become more prominent ~\cite{chen2024llava_mole,huang2024harder_task_need_more_expert .}
elements.sent-2411-017: To mitigate this, recent studies \cite{chen2024llava_mole,liu2024adamole incorporate the Mixture of Experts (MoE) paradigm into LoRA modules, embedding multiple LoRA units within a single linear layer so that each unit can leverage its specific strengths.}
elements.sent-2411-018: To better accommodate varied tasks, LoRA experts with different levels of granularity \cite{ or customized routing strategies \cite{liu2024adamole have been introduced.}}
elements.sent-2411-019: However, this complex design not only increases implementation complexity but may also extend the time required for training and inference.
elements.sent-2411-020: We thus raise the question: Is there a simple and unified approach to flexibly represent any group of LoRA experts?.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{imgs/teasaer.pdf} \caption{The two training stages of our framework. In the Vision Projector Pretraining stage, we incorporate the proposed Visual Cue Enhancement module (VCE) to enhance the final vision feature map by capturing detailed local information from multi-level visual feature maps. In the Visual Instruction Fine-Tuning stage, we use the proposed Dual Low-Rank Adaptation (Dual-LoRA) as the LLM adapter to learn task-specific knowledge while mitigating data conflicts, which is set trainable alone with VCE and vision projector.} \label{fig:training pipeline}
elements.sent-2411-021: In this paper, we address existing limitations by proposing Vision Cue Enhancement (VCE) and Dual Low-Rank Adaptation (Dual-LoRA) to improve the vision projector pretraining stage and the instruction finetuning stage respectively.
elements.sent-2411-022: An overview of our overall training pipeline is shown in Figure~\ref{fig:training pipeline .}
elements.sent-2411-023: For the VCE module, we extend feature capture to multi-level vision feature maps instead of relying solely on high-level semantic feature maps.
elements.sent-2411-024: Specifically, for each patch in the high-level feature map, we extract neighboring patches from the middle-layer feature maps and combine them with the original patch to create a locally enhanced feature patch.
elements.sent-2411-025: This approach enhances local visual cues while maintaining computational efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-026: To address data conflicts during MLLM instruction tuning, we introduce Dual-LoRA, a unified adaptation module that decouples learning into a skill low-rank space and a task-activation low-rank space.
elements.sent-2411-027: The skill space captures specific knowledge in downstream tasks, while the task space activates prior knowledge based on specific instructions.
elements.sent-2411-028: These two spaces are then jointly mapped into a cohesive single space to generate features for the subsequent stages.
elements.sent-2411-029: This separation of skill and task spaces within a single LoRA module enables fine-grained control over skill knowledge activation, allowing for dense LoRA rank learning of skill knowledge while supporting sparse, task-specific responses.
elements.sent-2411-030: Experiments on downstream tasks and general MLLM benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed method.
elements.sent-2411-031: Our contributions can be summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-032: We introduce a lightweight Visual Cue Enhancement (VCE) module to enrich fine-grained visual features for vision projector pretraining.
elements.sent-2411-033: We propose Dual Low-Rank Adaptation (Dual-LoRA), a simple yet efficient approach that mitigates data conflicts for effective Visual Instruction Fine-Tuning.
elements.sent-2411-034: Experimental results on both downstream tasks and general MLLM benchmarks validate the effectiveness of our proposed method.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{imgs/framework.pdf} \caption{Illustration of our proposed components. The Visual Cue Enhancement (VCE) module uses the projected vision feature map $f^{a}$ selected during pretraining as the anchor feature, leveraging nearby patches in multi-layer feature maps $[ f^{ref} ]$ to enrich the local visual cues of $f^{a}$. The Dual Low-Rank Adaptation decomposes the input feature into skill space and task space via $S$ and $T$, which are then multiplied element-wise, projected back to the same dimension as $x$ via $B$, added to the features obtained from the pretrained weights $W$, and passed to the next layer.} \label{fig:framework}
elements.sent-2411-035: Multi-Modal Large Language Models (MLLMs) integrate pretrained vision encoders with pretrained large language models (LLMs), showcasing remarkable instruction-following and generalization capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-036: Early models like Flamingo and BLIP-2 successfully adapted LLMs for visual tasks, demonstrating strong zero-shot generalization and in-context learning abilities.
elements.sent-2411-037: Visual instruction tuning, a key technique in recent studies~\cite{liu2023llava , has further enhanced MLLMs by using vision-language (VL) datasets and improving visual instruction-following data.}
elements.sent-2411-038: Additionally, some research approaches vision as a language, employing textual annotations (e.g., location descriptions of bounding boxes) to train MLLMs effectively.
elements.sent-2411-039: Enhancing Vision Feature Representation in MLLMs primarily involves vision encoder ensembling, resolution enhancement, and multi-level feature map fusion.
elements.sent-2411-040: For vision encoder ensembling, MMVP~\cite{tong2024MMVP utilizes a Mixture of Features (MoF) strategy to integrate image features from CLIP-ViT~\cite{radford2021clip and DINOv2~\cite{oquab2023dinov2 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-041: Similarly, MouSi~\cite{fan2024mousi uses an ensemble technique to leverage the strengths of individual vision encoders, introducing a fusion network to unify outputs from different encoders, such as CLIP, DINOv2, and SAM.}
elements.sent-2411-042: DeepSeekVL~\cite{lu2024deepseek_vl adopts a hybrid vision encoder design, encoding images by combining SigLIP-L for low-resolution inputs and SAM-B for high-resolution inputs.}
elements.sent-2411-043: LLaVA-UHD~\cite{xu2024llava0uhd organizes input images for efficient and scalable encoding, utilizing a compression module to further condense image tokens from visual encoders.}
elements.sent-2411-044: Some approaches focus on fusing multi-level feature maps.
elements.sent-2411-045: For example, Cambrian-1~\cite{tong2024cambrian1 introduces the Spatial Vision Aggregator, a dynamic, spatially-aware connector that integrates high-resolution visual features with LLMs while minimizing token count.}
elements.sent-2411-046: While most of these methods require full fine-tuning of the LLM, we propose a lightweight visual cue enhancer for more efficient fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-047: Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA)~\cite{hu2021lora introduces a method that freezes pretrained model weights and injects trainable low-rank decomposition matrices into the linear layers of the Transformer architecture, significantly reducing the number of trainable parameters required for downstream tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-048: Mixture of Experts in LoRA (MoE-LoRA) has gained popularity for addressing data conflicts in downstream task fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-049: MoE-LoRA typically employs three types of activation: sparse activation~\cite{chen2024llava_mole , where only the top-\( k \) experts are activated; dense activation~\cite{wu2024mixture_of_lora_experts , where all experts are activated and re-weighted by a router; and rectified activation~\cite{jiao2024rode , where experts are dynamically activated and optimized to be sparse.}}}
elements.sent-2411-050: For instance, LLaVA-MoLE selects only the top-1 expert for specific tasks.
elements.sent-2411-051: In dense activation, MoLE~\cite{wu2024mixture_of_lora_experts treats each trained LoRA module at each layer as an individual expert, integrating learnable gating functions at each layer to control hierarchical weights.}
elements.sent-2411-052: In rectified activation, RoDE~\cite{jiao2024rode designs diverse experts and employs a rectified linear unit to optimize the router for sparse expert activation.}
elements.sent-2411-053: These methods involve complex expert group configurations or routing strategies.
elements.sent-2411-054: In contrast, our approach offers a simple yet efficient adapter that effectively addresses data conflicts without requiring a complicated design.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth]{imgs/task_skill.pdf} \caption{Illustration of (a) VCE, (b) typical Mixture of Experts strategies for addressing data conflicts, and (c) Dual-LoRA. Mixture of Experts strategies include: Sparse activation~\cite{chen2024llava_mole}, where only a subset of experts is activated; dense activation~\cite{wu2024mixture_of_lora_experts}, where all experts contribute equally; and rectified activation~\cite{jiao2024rode}, where all experts contribute with selective rectification.} \label{fig:task_skill_space}

elements.sent-2411-061: Due to the large number of parameters in \( G \), optimizing \(\theta_{G}\) can be highly resource-intensive.
elements.sent-2411-062: To address this, LoRA~\cite{hu2021lora has been proposed as a lightweight adapter injected into the linear layers of LLMs.}
elements.sent-2411-063: LoRA significantly reduces the number of trainable parameters by decomposing the transformation of the input feature into a low-rank form.
elements.sent-2411-064: The LoRA module is represented by two parameter matrices, \( A \) and \( B \) with the same rank \(r\).
elements.sent-2411-065: In the \(i\)-th injected layer, let \( W_i \) denote the pre-trained weights and \( x_i \) denote the input feature for this layer; then the output after applying the LoRA module can be expressed as: \begin{equation}\label{eq:lora} z_i = W_i x + \frac{r}{\alpha } B_i A_i x,\end{equation} where \( z_i \) is the output feature passed to the next layer, and \(\alpha\) is a scaling factor.

elements.sent-2411-068: We propose a two-stage approach for efficient Visual Instruction Tuning: (1) Enhanced Vision Projector Pretraining and (2) Dual Low-Rank Adaptation (Dual-LoRA) Visual Instruction Fine-Tuning, as illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig:training pipeline .}

elements.sent-2411-091: Proposition 1: For a set of LoRAs \( \{ B_k A_k \}_{k=1 ^K \), each with rank 1, where \( K \) is the total number of LoRAs, the representation space of a single LoRA with rank \( K \) contains, and is at least as expressive as, the union of the representation spaces of the individual rank-1 LoRAs.}
elements.sent-2411-092: Corollary 1: For a set of LoRAs \( \{ B_k A_k \}_{k=1 ^K \), the combined representation capability does not exceed that of a single LoRA with rank \( \sum \text{rank (\text{LoRA _k) \).}}}

elements.sent-2411-173: In this paper, we explore efficient visual instruction fine-tuning for multi-modal large language models (MLLMs) by leveraging pre-trained models and utilizing only a few adapter parameters.
elements.sent-2411-174: Visual instruction fine-tuning of MLLMs faces two key challenges: insufficient capture of visual cues and conflicts among various instruction tasks due to the limited adapter parameters.
elements.sent-2411-175: To address these challenges, we propose a framework comprising Vision Cues Enhancement (VCE) and Dual Low-Rank Adaptation (Dual-LoRA).
elements.sent-2411-176: For Vision cue enhancement, we use multi-level feature maps to enrich the extracted vision cues.
elements.sent-2411-177: For Dual Low-Rank Adaptation, we introduce a rectified skill-space mapping technique, enabling the model to learn complex knowledge and respond specifically to instructions.
elements.sent-2411-178: Dual Low-Rank Adaptation possesses the expressive power of any ensemble of experts with a combined rank lower than its own.
elements.sent-2411-179: Evaluations on both general visual instruction benchmarks and domain-specific benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed method.. { \bibliographystyle{ieeenat_fullname.}}

elements.section-2411-007: Proof of Proposition 1, Corollary 1, and Corollary 2
elements.sent-2411-229: Statement: For a set of LoRAs \( \{ B_k A_k \}_{k=1 ^K \), each with rank 1, where \( K \) is the total number of LoRAs, the representation space of a single LoRA with rank \( K \) contains, and is at least as expressive as, the union of the representation spaces of the individual rank-1 LoRAs.}
elements.sent-2411-230: Proof: Each rank-1 LoRA \( B_k A_k \) can be expressed as the outer product of two vectors (since rank 1 implies that both \( A_k \) and \( B_k \) are vectors).
elements.sent-2411-231: The combined effect of these LoRAs is given by the sum: \begin{equation}M = \sum_{k=1}^K B_k A_k.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-232: The rank of \( M \) satisfies: \begin{equation}rank(M) \leq \sum_{k=1}^K rank(B_k A_k) = K,\end{equation} since the sum of \( K \) rank-1 matrices has rank at most \( K \).
elements.sent-2411-233: Now, consider a single LoRA \( BA \) with \( \text{rank (BA) = K \).}
elements.sent-2411-234: This single LoRA can represent any linear combination of up to \( K \) linearly independent rank-1 matrices.
elements.sent-2411-235: Therefore, it can represent any matrix \( M \) that is the sum of \( K \) rank-1 matrices.
elements.sent-2411-236: Thus, the representation space of the single LoRA \( BA \) with rank \( K \) includes all possible combinations that can be formed by the individual rank-1 LoRAs \( \{ B_k A_k \} \).. \(\blacksquare\).
elements.sent-2411-237: Statement: For a set of LoRAs \( \{ B_k A_k \}_{k=1 ^K \), the combined representation capability does not exceed that of a single LoRA with rank \( r = \sum_{k=1 ^K \text{rank (B_k A_k) \).}}}
elements.sent-2411-238: Proof: For each LoRA matrix \( B_k A_k \), let its rank be \( r_k = \text{rank (B_k A_k) \).}
elements.sent-2411-239: By the rank decomposition theorem, each \( B_k A_k \) can be expressed as a sum of \( r_k \) rank-1 matrices.
elements.sent-2411-240: That is, \begin{equation}B_k A_k = \sum_{i=1}^{r_k} B_{k,i} A_{k,i},\end{equation} where \( B_{k,i A_{k,i \) are rank-1 matrices.}}
elements.sent-2411-241: Consider the sum of all the LoRA matrices: \begin{equation}M = \sum_{k=1}^K B_k A_k = \sum_{k=1}^K \sum_{i=1}^{r_k} B_{k,i} A_{k,i}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-242: The total number of rank-1 matrices in this sum is \( r = \sum_{k=1 ^K r_k \).}
elements.sent-2411-243: By Proposition 1, the set of rank-1 matrices \( \{ B_{k,i A_{k,i \} \) can be represented within the space of a single LoRA matrix \( BA \) of rank \( r \).}}

elements.sent-2411-247: Proof: Define \( M = A \odot \sigma(T) \), where \( A \in \mathbb{R ^{m \times n \) and \( \sigma(T) \) is defined as: \begin{equation}\sigma(T) = [[t_1]_m, [t_2]_m, \ldots, [t_n]_m],\end{equation} where \( [t_i]_m \) is a column vector of size \( m \), with all entries equal to \( t_i \).}}
elements.sent-2411-248: The rank of \( M \) depends on the structure of both \( \sigma(T) \) and \( A \): If \( t_i = 0 \) for certain indices, the corresponding columns of \( M \) are zero, reducing the rank of \( M \).
elements.sent-2411-249: Consequently, the rank of \( M \), denoted \( r' \), satisfies: \begin{equation}r' = rank(M) \leq \min(rank(A), rank(\sigma(T))).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-250: From Corollary 1, a LoRA group with a total rank \( r \) is at most as expressive as a single LoRA \( BA \) with rank \( r \).
elements.sent-2411-251: Similarly, a LoRA group with a total rank \( r' \) is at most as expressive as the configuration \( B(A \odot \sigma(T)) \), where \( \text{rank (M) = r' \).}
elements.sent-2411-252: Now consider decomposing \( B(A \odot \sigma(T)) \) into \( K \) LoRA groups: \begin{equation}B(A \odot \sigma(T)) \approx \sum_{k=1}^K B_k A_k,\end{equation} where \( \text{rank (B_k A_k) = r_k \).}
elements.sent-2411-253: By the additive nature of rank, the total rank satisfies: \begin{equation}\sum_{k=1}^K r_k \leq r' \leq r.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-254: Thus, if the total rank satisfies \( r \geq \sum_{k=1 ^K \text{rank (B_k A_k) \), the configuration \( B(A \odot \sigma(T)) \) can be decomposed into any LoRA group where the total rank does not exceed \( r \).. \(\blacksquare\).}}
elements.section-2411-008: Implementation Details of the Visual Cue Enhancement Module
elements.sent-2411-255: Implementation Details of Deformable Attention For the input to deformable attention, we use the anchor feature (extracted from the second-to-last feature map of the CLIP ViT-L image encoder) as the query embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-256: The key and value embeddings are sampled from points in multi-level reference feature maps, specifically obtained from the 2nd-to-last, 8th-to-last, 14th-to-last, and 20th-to-last layers of the CLIP ViT-L encoder.
elements.sent-2411-257: The number of sampled reference points matches the number of image patches (e.g., \( (336/14)^2 = 24^2 \) for LLaVA-1.5-7B) and is initially distributed uniformly across the feature map, following the methodology described in Deformable DETR~\cite{zhu2020deformable_detr .}

elements.sent-2411-270: Efficient Visual Instruction Fine-Tuning (EVIT) seeks to adapt Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) to downstream tasks with minimal computational overhead.
elements.sent-2411-271: However, as task diversity and complexity increase, EVIT faces significant challenges in resolving data conflicts.
elements.sent-2411-272: To address this limitation, we propose the Dual Low-Rank Adaptation (Dual-LoRA), a holistic-to-local framework that enhances the adapter's capacity to address data conflict through dual structural optimization.
elements.sent-2411-273: Specifically, we utilize two subspaces: a skill space for stable, holistic knowledge retention, and a rank-rectified task space that locally activates the holistic knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-274: Additionally, we introduce Visual Cue Enhancement (VCE), a multi-level local feature aggregation module designed to enrich the vision-language projection with local details.
elements.sent-2411-275: Our approach is both memory- and time-efficient, requiring only 1.16$\times$ the inference time of the standard LoRA method (with injection into the query and value projection layers), and just 73\% of the inference time of a 4-expert LoRA-MoE.
elements.sent-2411-276: Extensive experiments on various downstream tasks and general MLLM benchmarks validate the effectiveness of our proposed methods.
elements.sent-2411-277: Our project page are publicly available at \url{https://github.com/pengkun-jiao/Dual-LoRA.}
elements.figure-2411-022: [] \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{imgs/teaser.pdf} \caption{ The motivation behind the proposed approach. (a) Humans perceive visual signals from both holistic and localized perspectives, considering the overall image structure and local details. (b) Humans acquire holistic knowledge, with specific parts (localized) applied to particular tasks. For example, one might first acquire holistic knowledge of cooking and then apply specific knowledge, \eg ingredient recognition, to source ingredients. } \label{fig:teaser}
elements.sent-2411-278: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have demonstrated impressive performances across a wide range of tasks, showcasing versatility and effectiveness in diverse applications~\cite{jiao2025lumen,jiao2025gaseraser,jiao2024inha .}
elements.sent-2411-279: Existing MLLMs~\cite{liu2023llava,bai2023qwen_vl,liu2024llava1.5 typically integrate a pretrained Large Language Models (LLM) \cite{touvron2023llama,chiang2023vicuna with a pretrained vision encoder \cite{radford2021clip,liu2023dino , using a vision projector to connect the vision encoder and LLM.}}}
elements.sent-2411-280: Vision Instruction Tuning~\cite{liu2023llava enables the practical application of MLLMs in downstream tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-281: However, full fine-tuning MLLMs can be highly resource-intensive.
elements.sent-2411-282: Efficient Vision Instruction Tuning (EVIT) addresses this challenge by tuning only the vision feature projector while integrating a small number of additional trainable parameters (adapters) into the LLM~\cite{ding2023peft .}
elements.sent-2411-283: For example, Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA)~\cite{hu2021lora leverages low-rank approximations to replace full fine-tuning of LMM, significantly reducing computational overhead.}
elements.figure-2411-023: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{imgs/task_skill.pdf} \caption{Comparison between the proposed Dual-LoRA and existing methods. (a) Mainstream MLLMs, \eg LLaVA~\cite{liu2023llava} and Qwen-VL~\cite{bai2023qwen_vl}, project the high-level visual feature map. (b) Our Visual Cue Enhancement module enhances high-level features by aggregating local information from multi-level feature maps. LoRA-MoE methods mitigate data conflicts by enabling localized responses, i.e., experts activation, tailored to several activation strategies: (c) Sparse Activation\cite{chen2024llava_mole}, where only the top-k experts are activated; (d) Dense Activation\cite{wu2024mixture_of_lora_experts}, where all experts are activated; and Rectified Activation~\cite{jiao2024rode}, where multiple heterogeneous experts are dynamically activated. In contrast, our (f) Dual Low-Rank Adaptation rectifies a holistic knowledge (skill) space with an additional task space, which is fully differentiable, capable of learning any local response, and more structurally efficient. } \label{fig:task_skill_space}
elements.sent-2411-284: However, existing EVIT methods face a critical limitation: as downstream tasks grow more diverse and complex, data conflicts in LoRA-based instruction tuning become increasingly pronounced \cite{chen2024llava_mole, huang2024harder_task_need_more_expert , ultimately impairing performance.}
elements.sent-2411-285: An intriguing observation is that when tuning for a variety of food-related tasks, LoRA fine-tuning can result in knowledge-inconsistent answers.
elements.sent-2411-286: For example, some ingredients may be misaligned between food ingredient recognition and recipe generation, as illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:quality_result .}
elements.sent-2411-287: To address this, recent studies suggest integrating LoRA within a Mixture of Experts (LoRA-MoE) paradigm~\cite{chen2024llava_mole,liu2024adamole,wu2024mixture_of_lora_experts .}
elements.sent-2411-288: LoRA-MoE adapts to diverse tasks by activating specific experts, which can be viewed as a process of localized adaptation, effectively mitigating data conflicts.
elements.sent-2411-289: However, the LoRA-MoE method requires a complex design to balance activation strategies, trainable parameters, and task complexity~\cite{jiao2024rode, huang2024harder_task_need_more_expert .}
elements.sent-2411-290: Moreover, it incurs significantly higher time consumption compared to vanilla LoRA when multiple LoRA experts are activated.
elements.sent-2411-291: To address the high time consumption of experts polling in LoRA-MoE, we propose a structurally efficient dual-space adaptation design that transitions from holistic domain knowledge learning to localized, task-specific adaptation.
elements.sent-2411-292: Additionally, we emphasize the role of localized adaptation in visual perception.
elements.sent-2411-293: A high-level overview of our motivation is illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig:teaser .}
elements.sent-2411-294: This design embodies a cognitively inspired learning paradigm: it first consolidates generalized skill representations and then dynamically routes task-critical knowledge components during inference, mirroring the transition in human cognition from holistic to localized processing.
elements.sent-2411-295: Specifically, we propose Dual Low-Rank Adaptation (Dual-LoRA), which strategically decouples adaptation learning into: 1) skill space learning for holistic domain knowledge acquisition, and 2) task space learning for instruction-conditioned task adaptation.
elements.sent-2411-296: The skill space is parameterized using a low-rank matrix optimized stably for cross-task knowledge consolidation, while the task space employs a rank-rectified matrix that dynamically modulates the skill space for localized task-specific adaptation.
elements.sent-2411-297: To enhance local details for vision feature projection, we further propose Visual Cue Enhancement (VCE), which enhances high-level visual features with local information aggregated from multiple feature maps.
elements.sent-2411-298: VCE remains computationally efficient by focusing on a limited number of local neighboring regions within each feature map.
elements.sent-2411-299: Figure~\ref{fig:task_skill_space illustrates the structurally efficient design of our approach.}
elements.sent-2411-300: Our VCE module is only 5.53 MB in size, and Dual-LoRA outperforms LoRA-MoE methods while using the same number of trainable parameters.
elements.sent-2411-301: The full approach requires just 1.16$\times$ the inference time of vanilla LoRA, and only 73\% of the inference time of 4-experts LoRA-MoE methods.
elements.sent-2411-302: Extensive experiments across a range of downstream tasks—including UniFood, ScienceQA, Flickr30k, and general MLLM benchmarks—demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed methods.
elements.sent-2411-303: Our main contributions can be summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-304: We design a time- and memory-efficient holistic-to-localized approach for EVIT.
elements.sent-2411-305: Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed methods.

elements.sent-2411-410: In this paper, we explore the transition from holistic to localized adaptation for efficient visual instruction fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-411: We introduce Dual Low-Rank Adaptation (Dual-LoRA), which enables the model to capture holistic knowledge while being locally activated for specific tasks, thereby mitigating data conflicts.
elements.sent-2411-412: Additionally, we propose Visual Cue Enhancement (VCE), which improves the vision feature projection by incorporating local information from multi-level vision feature maps.
elements.sent-2411-413: Our methods are both time- and memory-efficient, with only a 1.16$\times$ increase in time consumption compared to standard LoRA, yet significantly outperform LoRA in terms of performance with the same parameter settings.
elements.sent-2411-414: Extensive experiments on downstream tasks validate the effectiveness of our approach.
